danger_signals.visual_form: Enemies streaming in from four edges/passages of single-screen arena
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat phase; player must maintain constant vigilance and movement
Entire game is designed as lethal entertainment; survival is the core mechanic

danger_signals.visual_form: Designated boss arenas marked on level map after initial clearance
danger_signals.meaning: Escalated threat; narrative culmination of each level

safety_signals.visual_form: Exit doors/portals appear when all enemies defeated
safety_signals.meaning: Player has momentary safety; choice of progression path available

safety_signals.visual_form: Temporary weapons and power-ups scattered throughout arenas
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability indicates fair difficulty scaling; player agency in combat readiness

safety_signals.visual_form: Cash piles and gift-wrapped prizes visible on screen
safety_signals.meaning: Progress indicator; survival is generating reward; player is winning the 'game show'

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue (primary player identified as blue in arcade/SNES versions)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Protagonist in dystopian game show arena; distinct from enemy waves
Provides verbal interjections throughout gameplay; hosts the violent broadcast; direct opponent in final arena

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types enter from arena edges in organized waves
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Non-player hostile entities; no faction identity; purely mechanical threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemies streaming into arena from four passages; projectiles and movement; resource pickups visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies enter in waves; player must maintain dual-joystick movement and shooting control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemies eliminated; exit doors appear; level map displayed showing remaining arenas and boss location
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can choose exit path; no immediate threat; money/prizes collected update score state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dedicated boss arena reached via level map navigation; Game Show Host appears as final antagonist
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Escalated combat difficulty; final test before level completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bonus level accessible when sufficient keys collected throughout gameplay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player collects reward entities ('babes'); high-score opportunity; alternative progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 1999 future aesthetic; television/entertainment industrial environment; violent spectacle framing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Thematic tone: players are contestants in lethal entertainment; survival = success; host commentary reinforces 'big money, big prizes' reward structure

environmental_storytelling.example: Dystopian game show arena (borrowing from RoboCop/The Running Man 1987) communicates that violence is entertainment commodity; player is contestant, not soldier—rewards are consumer goods (VCRs, toasters, money) not military objectives
Phrases like 'Total carnage! I love it!', 'Big money! Big prizes!', 'I'd buy that for a dollar!' anchor gameplay to broadcast entertainment narrative; host is present authority/antagonist throughout

Map display after first arena shows overall level layout and boss location, communicating structured progression and inevitability of boss encounter; no backtracking allowed indicates one-way narrative flow

Scattered consumer goods and cash communicate dual success metric (survival + accumulation); two-player competitive reward at level end (most cash wins bonus) reinforces game show contestant framework

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Enter arena in organized waves from four edges; require constant player response; no tactical complexity implied—pure volume threat
Dual-joystick control (move in one direction, shoot in another simultaneously); collect power-ups and currency while evading/eliminating threats
Provides running commentary during gameplay; manifests as final boss opponent; embodies sadistic authority that frames entire game world as lethal entertainment
Appear only after arena emptied; present player choice (multiple exits available); lead to new arenas or boss encounter; no return to previous areas

notes: Super Smash TV is a twin-stick shooter set in a dystopian 1999 game show. World-state communication relies heavily on thematic framing (entertainment spectacle) rather than faction-based mechanics. The game communicates danger through wave-based enemy spawning and safety through resource availability and arena clearance. The Game Show Host serves as both thematic narrator and final antagonist, unifying the narrative around a dystopian media critique. No traditional faction system exists; instead, there is a contestant (player) vs. broadcast authority (host) structure. Visual communication is primarily diegetic (in-universe) through the game show conceit.
